question: Under UNCLOS Article 5, baselines follow the low-water line along the coast. Two competing doctrines address what happens when sea-level rise inundates the charted low-water marks of atoll states. The ambulatory doctrine holds that baselines must physically track the retreating shoreline, continuously shrinking maritime entitlements. The fixed-baseline doctrine—advocated by Pacific Island states before the ILC and ITLOS—holds that once-deposited baselines remain legally binding regardless of inundation. Compare these two doctrines. Your answer must address how the scientific characteristics of sea-level rise on atolls (including differential local subsidence, IPCC projected rates, and storm-surge compounding) bear on whether either doctrine can be operationalized, and which doctrine better preserves the EEZ sovereign rights and treaty obligations arising under UNCLOS Parts V and VI.
reference_answer: The ambulatory doctrine is textually faithful to Article 5's physical-feature anchor but is scientifically unworkable for atoll states: IPCC AR6 projects 0.3–1 m of global mean sea-level rise by 2100, compounded by local subsidence of 1–5 mm/yr on atolls like Tuvalu and the Marshall Islands, making continuous hydrographic resurveying financially and logistically impossible for these states. Operationalizing ambulatory baselines would cause progressive EEZ shrinkage, voiding fishing-license revenues and seabed resource rights that underpin national economies, and creating perverse incentives for larger states to contest entitlements on an annual basis. The fixed-baseline doctrine preserves EEZ continuity and honors outstanding treaty obligations—port access agreements, fisheries arrangements, and continental shelf submissions—but creates a legal fiction if basepoints are fully submerged, directly straining Article 121(3), which denies maritime zone entitlement to features unable to sustain habitation. The ILA's 2022 and 2024 resolutions and COSIS submissions to ITLOS (May 2024 Advisory Opinion) favor a presumption against ambulatory baselines for climate-affected states, provided inundation is attributable to anthropogenic forcing. This middle position is doctrinally superior: it severs entitlement from daily physical change while limiting abuse by requiring demonstrated climate causation.
required_facts: UNCLOS Article 5 ties normal baselines to the physical low-water line, creating a textual basis for ambulatory interpretation
IPCC AR6 projects 0.3–1 m global mean SLR by 2100; atoll subsidence of 1–5 mm/yr compounds eustatic rise locally
UNCLOS Article 121(3) withholds EEZ and continental shelf entitlements from submerged or uninhabitable rocks/features
The ILA (2022, 2024) and COSIS/ITLOS Advisory Opinion (May 2024) favor preserving baselines and entitlements for climate-affected small island states
critical_errors: Claiming that UNCLOS explicitly authorizes fixed baselines for all states regardless of cause of coastal retreat
Ignoring the operationalization problem of continuous hydrographic resurveying for resource-limited atoll states
Treating Article 121(3) as irrelevant to the fixed-baseline debate, overlooking that submerged features cannot generate maritime zones

must_cover: Articulates the scientific compounding of eustatic SLR with local atoll subsidence as a quantitative operationalization barrier for the ambulatory doctrine
Identifies Article 121(3) as the principal legal tension constraining the fixed-baseline doctrine when features are fully submerged
Explains that the fixed-baseline doctrine better preserves EEZ sovereign rights and treaty obligations but requires an anthropogenic-causation carve-out
nice_to_have: References ILA resolutions (2022 or 2024) or ITLOS May 2024 Advisory Opinion / COSIS proceedings
Distinguishes eustatic sea-level rise from local subsidence and explains why the distinction matters for legal attribution
automatic_checks: Answer references UNCLOS Article 5 by number or name
Answer references UNCLOS Article 121(3) by number or name
Answer identifies both a scientific operationalization problem and a legal doctrine problem
Answer takes a comparative position rather than describing each doctrine in isolation

provenance.quality_notes: Both domains are fully load-bearing: climate science is needed to evaluate operationalizability (SLR rates, subsidence, IPCC projections) and maritime law is needed to evaluate legal validity (Articles 5 and 121(3), EEZ entitlements, ILA and ITLOS doctrine). Neither domain alone can resolve the comparison—a pure maritime law answer cannot assess whether ambulatory resurveying is feasible, and a pure climate science answer cannot adjudicate which doctrine is legally superior.
